oai_response.prompt:
Fix a buggy program written in Python language to solve the following programming problem:
Description: Once Volodya was at the museum and saw a regular chessboard as a museum piece. And there were only four chess pieces on it: two white rooks, a white king and a black king. "Aha, blacks certainly didn't win!", — Volodya said and was right for sure. And your task is to say whether whites had won or not.Pieces on the chessboard are guaranteed to represent a correct position (every piece occupies one cell, no two pieces occupy the same cell and kings cannot take each other). Thus, your task is only to decide whether whites mate blacks. We would remind you that it means that the black king can be taken by one of the opponent's pieces at the moment and also it cannot move to an unbeaten position. A rook moves vertically or horizontally by any number of free cells (assuming there are no other pieces on its path), a king — to the adjacent cells (either by corner or by side). Certainly, pieces cannot leave the board. The black king might be able to take opponent's rooks at his turn (see sample 3).
Input Specification: The input contains 4 space-separated piece positions: positions of the two rooks, the white king and the black king. Each position on 8 × 8 chessboard is denoted by two symbols — ('a' - 'h') and ('1' - '8') — which stand for horizontal and vertical coordinates of the cell occupied by the piece. It is guaranteed, that no two pieces occupy the same cell, and kings cannot take each other.
Output Specification: Output should contain one word: "CHECKMATE" if whites mate blacks, and "OTHER" otherwise.

Sample Input:
a6 b4 c8 a8
Sample Output:
CHECKMATE

Sample Input:
a6 c4 b6 b8
Sample Output:
OTHER

Sample Input:
a2 b1 a3 a1
Sample Output:
OTHER

Notes: None
Take input from standard input and output to standard output

c[x1][y1] = 0

message.content:
```python
import sys

b = 'abcdefgh'
a1, a2, a3, a4 = map(str, input().split())
x1 = b.index(a1[0])
y1 = int(a1[1]) - 1
x2 = b.index(a2[0])
y2 = int(a2[1]) - 1
x3 = b.index(a3[0])
y3 = int(a3[1]) - 1
x4 = b.index(a4[0])
y4 = int(a4[1]) - 1

c = [[0 for _ in range(8)] for _ in range(8)]

# Mark rook attacks
for i in range(8):
    c[x1][i] = 1
for i in range(8):
    c[x2][i] = 1
for i in range(8):
    c[i][y1] = 1
for i in range(8):
    c[i][y2] = 1

# Unmark the positions of the rooks themselves
c[x1][y1] = 0
c[x2][y2] = 0

# Mark king attacks
for i in range(-1, 2):
    for j in range(-1, 2):
        if 0 <= x3 + i < 8 and 0 <= y3 + j < 8:
            c[x3 + i][y3 + j] = 1

# Check if black king is under attack (check)
if c[x4][y4] == 0:
    print("OTHER")
    sys.exit()

# Check all possible moves of the black king
for i in range(-1, 2):
    for j in range(-1, 2):
        if i == 0 and j == 0:
            continue
        nx, ny = x4 + i, y4 + j
        if 0 <= nx < 8 and 0 <= ny < 8:
            # Check if the new position is not under attack
            # Also, check if the new position is not occupied by a white piece
            if c[nx][ny] == 0:
                # Check if the new position is not occupied by a white piece
                if (nx == x1 and ny == y1) or (nx == x2 and ny == y2) or (nx == x3 and ny == y3):
                    continue
                print("OTHER")
                sys.exit()

print("CHECKMATE")
```

```python
import sys

b = 'sabcdefgh'
a1, a2, a3, a4 = map(str, input().split())
x1 = b.index(a1[0]) - 1
y1 = int(a1[1]) - 1
x2 = b.index(a2[0]) - 1
y2 = int(a2[1]) - 1
x3 = b.index(a3[0]) - 1
y3 = int(a3[1]) - 1
x4 = b.index(a4[0]) - 1
y4 = int(a4[1]) - 1

c = [[0 for _ in range(8)] for _ in range(8)]

# Mark rook1's attack lines
for i in range(8):
    c[x1][i] = 1
for i in range(8):
    c[i][y1] = 1

# Mark rook2's attack lines
for i in range(8):
    c[x2][i] = 1
for i in range(8):
    c[i][y2] = 1

# Unmark the positions of the rooks themselves
c[x1][y1] = 0
c[x2][y2] = 0

# Mark white king's attack squares
for i in range(-1, 2):
    for j in range(-1, 2):
        if 0 <= x3 + i < 8 and 0 <= y3 + j < 8:
            c[x3 + i][y3 + j] = 1

# Check if black king is in check
if c[x4][y4] == 0:
    print("OTHER")
    sys.exit()

# Check if all adjacent squares are under attack
for i in range(-1, 2):
    for j in range(-1, 2):
        if i == 0 and j == 0:
            continue
        nx, ny = x4 + i, y4 + j
        if 0 <= nx < 8 and 0 <= ny < 8:
            # Check if the square is not under attack or can be captured by taking a rook
            if c[nx][ny] == 0:
                # Check if the square is occupied by a rook and can be captured
                if (nx == x1 and ny == y1) and not (abs(nx - x3) <= 1 and abs(ny - y3) <= 1):
                    continue
                elif (nx == x2 and ny == y2) and not (abs(nx - x3) <= 1 and abs(ny - y3) <= 1):
                    continue
                else:
                    print("OTHER")
                    sys.exit()

print("CHECKMATE")
```

source_data.prob_desc_input_spec: The input contains 4 space-separated piece positions: positions of the two rooks, the white king and the black king. Each position on 8 × 8 chessboard is denoted by two symbols — ('a' - 'h') and ('1' - '8') — which stand for horizontal and vertical coordinates of the cell occupied by the piece. It is guaranteed, that no two pieces occupy the same cell, and kings cannot take each other.
source_data.prob_desc_output_spec: Output should contain one word: "CHECKMATE" if whites mate blacks, and "OTHER" otherwise.
source_data.prob_desc_description: Once Volodya was at the museum and saw a regular chessboard as a museum piece. And there were only four chess pieces on it: two white rooks, a white king and a black king. "Aha, blacks certainly didn't win!", — Volodya said and was right for sure. And your task is to say whether whites had won or not.Pieces on the chessboard are guaranteed to represent a correct position (every piece occupies one cell, no two pieces occupy the same cell and kings cannot take each other). Thus, your task is only to decide whether whites mate blacks. We would remind you that it means that the black king can be taken by one of the opponent's pieces at the moment and also it cannot move to an unbeaten position. A rook moves vertically or horizontally by any number of free cells (assuming there are no other pieces on its path), a king — to the adjacent cells (either by corner or by side). Certainly, pieces cannot leave the board. The black king might be able to take opponent's rooks at his turn (see sample 3).
